title: Opioid Analgesics and Naloxone
subtitle: The nurse gives opioids safely — watching for sedation before respiratory depression, holding for a slow respiratory rate, preventing constipation, and keeping naloxone ready.

patient.name: Opioid Analgesics
patient.admitDate: The nurse safely administers and monitors opioid pain medication.

labs.v: Hold if RR < 12 or very drowsy.

nursesNote: The nurse safely administers opioid analgesics (such as morphine, hydromorphone, oxycodone, fentanyl) for moderate to severe pain. Because opioids are central nervous system depressants, the nurse assesses the respiratory rate and the level of sedation before each dose and holds the dose, then notifies the provider, if the respiratory rate is below 12 or the patient is overly sedated and hard to arouse. The key safety principle is that increasing sedation comes before respiratory depression, so a rising sedation level is the early warning sign to act on, not just a low respiratory rate. The most serious adverse effect is respiratory depression (slow, shallow breathing), and the antidote naloxone is kept readily available; the nurse should know that naloxone is short-acting, so the opioid effect and pain can return and repeat doses and continued monitoring may be needed. Constipation is very common, does not improve with continued use, and is prevented with a scheduled bowel regimen, fluids, and fiber. Nausea and itching are common early and usually ease, while dizziness and orthostatic hypotension call for fall precautions. The nurse reassesses pain after giving the medication, uses the lowest effective dose, and teaches the patient and family about safe use, sedation warning signs, and not combining opioids with alcohol or other sedatives.
providerOrders: Assess respiratory rate and sedation level before each opioid dose
Hold the dose and notify the provider if the respiratory rate is under 12
Keep naloxone available for opioid-induced respiratory depression
Start a bowel regimen to prevent constipation
Use fall precautions and reassess pain after the dose

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about opioid analgesics?

opts.text: Check respiratory rate and sedation before each dose
Hold the dose if the respiratory rate is below 12
Increasing sedation can warn of respiratory depression
Naloxone is the antidote for opioids
Constipation goes away with continued use
Opioids can cause dizziness and low blood pressure
Respiratory depression is the most serious effect
A bowel regimen helps prevent constipation
Naloxone is long-acting, so one dose is always enough
Combining opioids with alcohol increases the risk
Pain should be reassessed after giving the dose
Hard-to-arouse sedation is a reason to hold the opioid

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order giving a scheduled opioid for pain

opts.text: Assess pain, respiratory rate, and sedation level
Confirm the rate is 12 or above and patient is arousable
Give the dose and keep naloxone available
Reassess pain and respiratory status
Document and address constipation prevention

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Give the dose or Hold and notify?

opts.text: Respiratory rate 16 and alert with pain
Respiratory rate 8 and hard to arouse
Patient rates pain 7/10, awake, RR 18
Very drowsy and falling asleep mid-sentence
Stable vitals, no excess sedation
Pinpoint pupils, RR 7, barely responsive

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: After a dose, RR is 6 and the patient is barely arousable
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Stimulate the patient and open the airway
Apply oxygen and support breathing
Give naloxone as ordered
Notify the provider and Rapid Response
Keep monitoring; pain and sedation can return
Checking respiratory rate before each opioid dose
Giving the next dose when RR is 8
Starting a bowel regimen with the opioid
Telling the patient to drink alcohol for extra relief
Keeping naloxone readily available
Assuming one naloxone dose lasts all day
Using fall precautions for dizziness
Ignoring rising sedation if the rate is still 12

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Pharmacological and Parenteral Therapies, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
